Speaker 6: Thanks for allowing me time just to give you a quick update on where we are as a sector for tourism and particularly for Waipā District.

Speaker 7: I see Sam nodding.

Speaker 6: Good morning, or good afternoon, sorry everyone.
Speaker 6: Thanks for allowing me time just to give you a quick update on where we are as a sector for tourism and particularly for Waipā District.
Speaker 6: So a report has obviously been provided to you and prepared which was our last six months performance at the end of 31st December 2019.
Speaker 6: As you would have seen, Waipā District Council was going through significant growth, probably one of the fastest growing
Speaker 6: regions for us with regards to tourism impact.
Speaker 6: You were experiencing double digit growth pretty much through visitor expenditure and also commercial accommodation.
Speaker 6: But we are here today to discuss just obviously a bit of a change in the dynamic given C O V I D and the impact for tourism particularly as a sector.
Speaker 6: Go to the next slide Sam.
Speaker 6: Perfect.
Speaker 6: So just first a couple of slides obviously just covering off some data.
Speaker 6: We are now receiving international and national data on a weekly basis from the government and also from the airlines.
Speaker 6: So no surprise obviously since C O V I D lockdown and international travel restrictions were put in place, international visitor arrivals into New Zealand have been dropping at a very sharp rate.
Speaker 6: We still do have airline connectivity which I will go through.
Speaker 6: Because we are still maintaining what we call a baseline service for connections across the world, mainly for repatriation flights, but also for cargo and service across the country as well.
Speaker 6: Next slide, Sam.
Speaker 6: So weekly card transactions, again, no surprise, obviously, overall, our visitor expenditure is down significantly.
Speaker 6: But when you do look on to the left, top five, obviously, food, liquor and pharmacies, we are still seeing some cumulative and some obviously weekly growth in expenditure compared to all our other visitor type expenditure and transactions as well.
Speaker 6: Thanks, Sam.
Speaker 6: So the impact for us as a sector, again, look, I mean, all of this has been reported.
Speaker 6: But just to cover off in summary, we were already impacted through the loss of China group travel, which started in the second to last week of January.
Speaker 6: So with that, all of our international facing products, our hotels, our motels, our Airbnbs were immediately impacted by that because many of China group travel actually did stay in the Waikato.
Speaker 6: They were our fourth largest international visitor market.
Speaker 6: behind Australia, North America and U K and Europe.
Speaker 6: It did shut us down pretty much once international travel restrictions hit us and the visitor economy covers more than just the tourism operators again we have mentioned accommodation it also impacts retail, hospitality, transport providers, our operators of course but also event organisers and major venues and of course Mystery Creek, Avanti Drome and Lake Harapero are key event platforms for Waipā District.

Speaker 6: behind Australia, North America and U K and Europe.
Speaker 6: It did shut us down pretty much once international travel restrictions hit us and the visitor economy covers more than just the tourism operators again we have mentioned accommodation it also impacts retail, hospitality, transport providers, our operators of course but also event organisers and major venues and of course Mystery Creek, Avanti Drome and Lake Harapero are key event platforms for Waipā District.
Speaker 6: We have had significant job losses and business closures across tourism.
Speaker 6: I have down underneath that last bullet point, look, the immediate business impact that we have been coaching our businesses through and helping them get through.
Speaker 6: Some have gone into what we call temporary or seasonal closure.
Speaker 6: So again, we have a lot of seasonality in tourism.
Speaker 6: many who would close for winter have closed but those that potentially have never closed before we have suggested they go into hibernation and so that is what we are calling temporary seasonal closures we have had a number of businesses you know hobbiton was obviously one of the most public ones reported in the media but again all our motels hotels retail hospitality
Speaker 6: have pretty much reduced service and staff there is only essential services operating as we know but again with alert level three we do see some opening up potentially in the hospitality sector for Waipā anyway and then the last obviously implication for us is we have had a number of businesses who are permanently closing not only are these a loss definitely to the tourism industry to try and bring people back when we do not have products
Speaker 6: But also particularly for those vulnerable communities where some of these operators have been going for many years.
Speaker 6: Thanks Sam.
Speaker 5: Can I ask a question about that?
Speaker 5: Sure.
Speaker 5: Have you got examples of what businesses are struggling and who is closing and how it is impacting around the wider industry?
Speaker 6: Yeah, so particularly for Y P A R, it has mainly been your hospitality, potentially some of your retail and also accommodation.
Speaker 6: So we have had many, nearly all of your accommodation has pretty much gone into hibernation or closure.
Speaker 6: They are calling it temporary closure, but some of them have been able to access the wage subsidy, which is great.
Speaker 6: But again, what we are going to see is the impacts are longer than the twelve week wage subsidy right now.
Speaker 6: So it is post twelve weeks, what we are quite concerned about.
Speaker 6: We have been lobbying nationally, the government, and I think that is all sectors, not just tourism, around potentially extending the wage subsidy for twenty weeks, because it will take a lot to restart the tourism sector again.
Speaker 6: People's propensity to travel is quite low.
Speaker 6: We will travel locally and potentially we will travel intra-regionally, so staying within the region, but to try and stand up the domestic travel market is going to be a long haul.

Speaker 6: We have been lobbying nationally, the government, and I think that is all sectors, not just tourism, around potentially extending the wage subsidy for twenty weeks, because it will take a lot to restart the tourism sector again.
Speaker 6: People's propensity to travel is quite low.
Speaker 6: We will travel locally and potentially we will travel intra-regionally, so staying within the region, but to try and stand up the domestic travel market is going to be a long haul.
Speaker 6: We are looking at a six to twelve months recovery plan for that.
Speaker 6: Great.
Speaker 6: So lastly, look, I mean, no surprise here, this was just something we do for industries.
Speaker 6: People know where we all fit.
Speaker 6: So from the tourism perspective, when we respond, obviously, we are providing industry support right now at a national level, regional level, and also
Speaker 6: at a local level but then we have Te Waka, our chambers of commerce, our business associations and all local government have been great in actually supporting us with what we call the business and community support side for tourism businesses and then lastly we are obviously partnered with the Civil Defence Network around providing welfare support not just for tourism operators again it is also been utilising tourism operators many of our caterers have now been repurposed into providing
Speaker 6: food services pretty much or supporting the food banks and actually cooking meals.
Speaker 6: We also have a number of our transport operators and tourism being basically requisitioned now to provide transport options and actually getting people around such as essential workers and many of the staff potentially we have been redeploying into the Civil Defence Network through providing security and welfare and call centre as well.
Speaker 6: So not only are we also working with Civil Defence we are also putting staff into there as well.
Speaker 6: Thanks Sam.
Speaker 6: Even though we are obviously dealing with C O V I D, this is just for you to be aware that
Speaker 6: expressway and some of the other work that potentially has been put forward for shovel ready even extending the expressway down to Piariri that is just all going to help the ease of domestic travel and we definitely see the push from self-drive obviously the motorhome and caravan association they are going to be another first leader and mover back into the region as well which is fantastic so we are working with them and A A Traveller.
Speaker 6: around trying to get more people back into Waipā.
Speaker 6: So I think, again, your geographic location, your natural environment and also your event platforms hold you in a stronger position around obviously recovering compared to other parts of our region.
Speaker 7: Great.
Speaker 7: O K. Thanks very much for that.
Speaker 7: You have done an awful lot of work and you have got a lot more in front of you, I can see.
Speaker 7: But that is great.

Speaker 7: All the best.

Speaker 4: Thanks Andrew and thanks for the opportunity.
Speaker 4: Welcome to all of the elected members.
Speaker 4: Hopefully we have got a presentation coming up and that should be before you now.
Speaker 4: Look, today I have also got Scott Kendall, my finance manager.
Speaker 4: Hi everyone.
Speaker 4: So Scott will deal with a couple of the finance slides and as most of what we have to talk to you about is in the realms of finance, useful to have them here.
Speaker 4: Perhaps not quite the presentation I had intended to present a month ago, but as we know a lot has changed and a massive amount has changed for Rau in the last three or four weeks.
Speaker 4: So the presentation is primarily, and if we can Sam move to the next slide, is primarily around the C O V I D-nineteen response for Rau.
Speaker 4: But I thought, and there is about eight or nine slides, key slides to take you through.
Speaker 4: And I think what you will see is how we have dealt with the crisis.
Speaker 4: what the implications have been for the group now and for the next fifteen months and a summary that sheds some positive light at the end of the tunnel.
Speaker 4: Thanks Sam if we move on.
Speaker 4: So I guess the first point I think is useful too, you have obviously received our six-month interim results
Speaker 4: Some say you are only as good as your last results, but sadly that is not quite the case today.
Speaker 4: But look, we were travelling extremely well, very good first six months, and in fact on target for a record financial performance under any measure of our S I I for this financial year.
Speaker 4: But of course and that led to our draft S O I that you have also received and that of course is now pretty much irrelevant and I see that you ironically have an appendix letter to my chair Barry Harris about our S O I and as you can imagine we are working very quickly towards a revised S O I for the next three years.

Speaker 4: But of course and that led to our draft S O I that you have also received and that of course is now pretty much irrelevant and I see that you ironically have an appendix letter to my chair Barry Harris about our S O I and as you can imagine we are working very quickly towards a revised S O I for the next three years.
Speaker 4: particularly the financials, but also there will be some narrative change and some priority changing as well.
Speaker 4: We have, as I say, completed a very detailed fifteen-month forecast through to thirty June next year.
Speaker 4: That will form, of course, now part of the year one of the S O I.
Speaker 4: So look, we have a presentation.
Speaker 4: The Rail Board and myself have a presentation to all shareholding.
Speaker 4: tēnā katoa tēnā tēnā tēnā tēnā tēnā tēnā tēnā tēnā tēnā tēnā tēnā tēnā tēnā
Speaker 4: So I think now just looking at the impact and look very difficult to, well it is very easy to explain in simple terms, it is had a massive impact on the business but if I divide it into these four areas that are before you.
Speaker 4: Of course, the first is the aeronautical business.
Speaker 4: In New Zealand, complete, as you know, suspension of flights.
Speaker 4: So for us, that was an immediate loss of income, really from April, but from the last week of March.
Speaker 4: And along with our general aviation, L3 Harris, the flight school, basically, you know, ninety seven, ninety eight loss of our aeronautical income overnight.
Speaker 4: Terminal closed and we are really operating as an emergency medical service only at the airport.
Speaker 4: Had the same impact on our property, but in fact our property is our lifeline that I will come to.
Speaker 4: But again, really overnight impact on our tenancies.
Speaker 4: We are quite a large landlord as you know.
Speaker 4: But to date, and Scott will talk a wee bit about this, but to date we have had good support from our tenants, but we are early days, as we know, into the next sort of three to six months as businesses work through how they are going to operate and survive during this process.
Speaker 4: We have obviously suspended the terminal upgrade work, and I will talk to that a bit further on, which of course was a major piece of work, approximately fifteen million of proposed work.
Speaker 4: A hotel has been, sorry if we can just go back to the previous slide Sam, the hotel impacted significantly as well.
Speaker 4: Fortunately during April it was taken over by Ministry of Health as a isolation facility.
Speaker 4: It was not required, it was a backup to the Auckland hotels.
Speaker 4: Nonetheless we have received and we are receiving payment for the use of the hotel
Speaker 4: by the government, but that ends really this week and from then on at difficult times ahead and we will talk about that.

Speaker 4: Fortunately during April it was taken over by Ministry of Health as a isolation facility.
Speaker 4: It was not required, it was a backup to the Auckland hotels.
Speaker 4: Nonetheless we have received and we are receiving payment for the use of the hotel
Speaker 4: by the government, but that ends really this week and from then on at difficult times ahead and we will talk about that.
Speaker 4: And obviously our people, all my team are working remotely.
Speaker 4: That is working very, very well.
Speaker 4: My sort of eleven or twelve on our immediate C O V I D team response.
Speaker 4: But of course, the cafe and store staff are at home, unable to work and part of the government subsidy.
Speaker 4: Our rescue fire team does remain on site at this point.
Speaker 4: So that is really been the impact.
Speaker 4: What have we really tried to do about it?
Speaker 4: If we could move to the next slide.
Speaker 4: First of all, we very quickly leading into
Speaker 4: Probably two weeks before we moved into level two and level three, or before we even knew what that meant, we had worked very closely with the board around a pandemic plan, how we would operate the business, and more importantly, started to look at some scenario and modelling at that point.
Speaker 4: As Jason would have mentioned earlier on, as borders such as China were closing to tourism, we also saw the writing on the ward.
Speaker 4: on the wall for our own business.
Speaker 4: So that plan was developed and approved by the board before we even went into lockdown.
Speaker 4: We have completed significant cost reductions in the business, that is right across the business, so that is major cost reductions in our expense lines, reductions pretty much across our business in terms of payroll, anything up to a twenty reduction, and the board have also taken a reduction in their director fees.
Speaker 4: We applied for and received the government wage subsidy across the group.
Speaker 4: And with the hotel, we worked through and are still working through with Jet Park.
Speaker 4: But they have gone through a full restructuring exercise.
Speaker 4: A number of people have been made redundant.
Speaker 4: A number have been retained under the government subsidy.
Speaker 4: But we have a meeting later this week with Jet Park to look at stage two.
Speaker 4: The airport association that we are part of is doing a major piece of work with central government to try and see what support, if any, there are for airport companies that have been significantly hit.
Speaker 4: As you know, we are long-term infrastructure owners and that requires significant cash and capital to maintain the assets.
Speaker 4: So there is a piece of work going on in that space.
Speaker 4: We have already covered the people, we can move to the next slide.
Speaker 4: The scenario modelling that I talk about there, I think the point that I will make here and come back to at the end is that

Speaker 4: As you know, we are long-term infrastructure owners and that requires significant cash and capital to maintain the assets.
Speaker 4: So there is a piece of work going on in that space.
Speaker 4: We have already covered the people, we can move to the next slide.
Speaker 4: The scenario modelling that I talk about there, I think the point that I will make here and come back to at the end is that
Speaker 4: Most airport companies, particularly regional airport companies, are significantly hit and hit now.
Speaker 4: We are fortunate that we made the decision five or six years ago that the board, then under the leadership of John Spencer and then implemented by myself and my team, we have, as you know, diversified the airport company.
Speaker 4: And that is, I guess, bought us time.
Speaker 4: So we have completed a very conservative model for argument's sake.
Speaker 4: It assumes no Air New Zealand activity of any meaningful sense before October this year.
Speaker 4: And then only a recovery of about forty of passenger numbers through 2021 calendar year and well into 2022 before we have a positive E B I T D A really.
Speaker 4: So what is, and we have also taken a very conservative view of our leases, of our lessees' ability to pay us, etc.
Speaker 4: No activity out of the hotel really for the balance of this calendar year.
Speaker 4: So very, very conservative but realistic modelling.
Speaker 4: Fortunately we have a major land sale that is unconditional and
Speaker 4: a very, extremely good family, significant family, multinational business that have agreed to further provide a letter of credit to the airport company, which gives me confidence to build the road, so to speak.
Speaker 4: So that delivers about 2.5 million of margin of free cash flow.
Speaker 4: into the group early next year.
Speaker 4: So the reality is that we can get through this year and through to June twenty one almost a slightly positive cash position.
Speaker 4: The balance sheet's taken a bit of a hit and Scott will take you through that.
Speaker 4: And also we have some work going on on Central Precinct and again we have the ability hopefully to settle some unconditional land sales with the same degree of confidence around further sureties.
Speaker 4: which will also feed some additional cash into the group.
Speaker 4: We have only utilised about sixty five of our debt funding, and in fact, we have further facilities that we were intending to take anyway pre-C O V I D, and we will still take, which will take our funding up to about thirty million.
Speaker 4: And then we will have only utilised not much more than fifty of that by June next year.
Speaker 4: So most of the cash burn is funded out of our overdraft during this period and then the land sales kick back in and we are back in positive space.

Speaker 4: And then we will have only utilised not much more than fifty of that by June next year.
Speaker 4: So most of the cash burn is funded out of our overdraft during this period and then the land sales kick back in and we are back in positive space.
Speaker 4: Being said, very supportive and no issues there around funding.
Speaker 4: Next slide, please.
Speaker 4: So we have joined with Hamilton City and I have spoken to Gary Dyett and others around obviously the Crown infrastructure projects.
Speaker 4: We have put forward two applications if you have had a chance to review the submission.
Speaker 4: As you would be aware, there are twenty three projects we have submitted for the terminal redevelopment.
Speaker 4: And also for an amalgam of a number of projects around the airport that deal with roading, lateral infrastructure, our wastewater plant and various other aspects that again is also worth about eleven million.
Speaker 4: So we have
Speaker 4: put those in.
Speaker 4: Whether we are successful, who knows, but under the terminal redevelopment project, we are ready to go.
Speaker 4: Tenders have been received and being evaluated the week of lockdown.
Speaker 4: Our consent is in with your council at the moment, and that was well underway.
Speaker 4: So it is a shovel-ready project.
Speaker 4: We could be out of the ground and doing enabling works within a month of a funding line.
Speaker 4: The key for myself and the board will be what are the tags that the government apply if we are successful to the funding mechanism, how much of it is a loan, is a grant, etc.
Speaker 4: That is underway.
Speaker 4: And that is part of our recovery programme.
Speaker 4: So if we go to the next slide.
Speaker 4: It is been important as we have worked through really the devastation of the past three or four weeks to make sure that we still keep our eye on the longer term for the group and our focus over the next twelve months.
Speaker 4: I will not spend any detailed time here other than to let you know that we have a clear business plan and a clear set of objectives for FY21.
Speaker 4: Most of those remain in place.
Speaker 4: We are reviewing them currently as a management team, but we are trying to make sure that we continue.
Speaker 4: Property is a major contributor, and as you can see, they are potentially just shy of eight million in land sales in this next twelve-month period.
Speaker 4: operations we continue to maintain the runway fortunately in our ten-year asset management plan we have a very quiet we just spent close to a million dollars finishing in March of this year on some apron works and some pumps some other bits and pieces but we have a very quiet capital expenditure for aeronautical over the next

Speaker 4: operations we continue to maintain the runway fortunately in our ten-year asset management plan we have a very quiet we just spent close to a million dollars finishing in March of this year on some apron works and some pumps some other bits and pieces but we have a very quiet capital expenditure for aeronautical over the next
Speaker 4: twelve to eighteen months, which could not have come at a better time.
Speaker 4: But we maintain the assets and you need to know that we will.
Speaker 4: When it comes to, we will be working very closely with Air New Zealand to try and obviously kickstart services again.
Speaker 4: And so our whole marketing and social media and comms strategy will be around exactly that.
Speaker 4: A hotel is a much more difficult beast as you would have gained from Jason's presentation.
Speaker 4: But look, we are working now closely with Jet Park to understand what a plan coming out the other side may look like.
Speaker 4: Fortunately, it is a hotel that was not reliant on international tourism, but it is reliant on conferencing.
Speaker 4: in particular, and we are yet to understand the behaviour of the business community as we go forward in this new world.
Speaker 4: So we have just almost spending close to four million on the hotel just prior to lockdown.
Speaker 4: So it is a property that we are going to have to maintain in the meantime and fund, and our conservative modelling does mean that the group can fund its interests in the hotel, certainly for the next eighteen months with limited activity from it.
Speaker 4: And if we move now into the next slide, which is probably the financial, a couple of slides here on sort of financials, and I will let Scott lead in now.
Speaker 4: Our overarching comment here would be, you had a lot of detail in the six-month interim results, so we decided there was no point in replicating information you will have read.
Speaker 4: So Scott will summarise it here, and more importantly, take you out the other side over the next couple of years.
Speaker 3: Good afternoon everyone and thanks Mark for the introduction.
Speaker 3: Yeah I will not dwell too much on the past because it is unfortunately become largely irrelevant but I guess just some key takeaways from the slide and what you may have read already in the interim report but really that momentum we have built in the past couple of years around a sustainable so recurring revenue base through passenger growth in the airport business through our property portfolio that it continued really well for the first half of the year.
Speaker 3: and you know obviously without C O V I D-nineteen we expected to continue through the full year and beyond yeah just some points around property obviously talking some big numbers around land sales I guess that they are land sales that have been contemplated under our ten-year plan the land sales in the pipeline it is not something that we have done there is a knee-jerk reaction or sort of a fire sale type arrangement just to get some cash for the next couple of years

Speaker 3: The land we are selling is sort of surplus to aeronautical requirements.
Speaker 3: In fact, much of it is actually physically separated from the airport through a gulley system.
Speaker 3: So we are not compromising any core assets, sort of future capability by selling this land.
Speaker 3: It very much is land that was surplus to the airport.
Speaker 3: So it is why it is being sold off at the time it has been.
Speaker 3: Yeah, just with the hotel, obviously it is a new investment for us, a new industry we have invested in.
Speaker 3: I am really pleased with the early trading performance.
Speaker 3: Yeah, and probably pleasing that we did not, other than the initial money to do the property up, attend to some deferred maintenance and things, it is actually traded cash flow positive.
Speaker 3: It has not been profitable with depreciation and things, but we are actually able to repay some debt.
Speaker 3: And, you know, the early vital signs of inoccupancy and room rate have been
Speaker 3: certainly in line with the expectation and again kind of right up until February March it was really just coming into a stride around the conference centre and the food and beverage offerings so yeah I think you know it was yeah it was looking really positive and hopefully on the other side that is the momentum we can we can leverage off again so probably just look look to the future now and just move to the next slide please
Speaker 3: So I have given here just sort of in six-month increments the remainder of the financial year, also the financial year that we are in, and then the following three that are presented in the draft statement of the tent you have received.
Speaker 3: I think important to focus on E B I T D A because that is sort of a cash flow metric for us.
Speaker 3: That includes land sales, but it excludes, of course, costs like depreciation, which is around three million a year for us, which in the short term is not necessarily
Speaker 3: with the investment we make into assets.
Speaker 3: So obviously seeing a reasonably significant decline in this calendar year across the business certainly going to suffer.
Speaker 3: And that is, as we see, in the sort of early six months between December twenty and June next year, that is the effect of this large land sale taking effect.
Speaker 3: And then as the business kind of gets back on its feet,

Speaker 3: with the investment we make into assets.
